title: Strange New World: Lecture 2
speaker: Dr. Carl Trueman
source_files: WRS 2021 Ron Taber Lecture Series_ Strange New World - Carl Trueman 2 of 4.txt
outline: Introduction: Responsible groups that shaped modern identity
The role of Romanticism and psychoanalysis
The sexual revolution and its philosophical roots
How these movements transformed public life
The church's response to the changing culture
summary: Dr. Carl Trueman continues the lecture series, examining specific responsible groups and movements that have shaped the modern understanding of identity. He conducts a 'board experiment' to illustrate cultural shifts in how individuals understand themselves.
transcript:
## The Core Question: From Inner Life to Identity

I want to begin with a thought experiment. Imagine going to a doctor eighty-five years ago and saying: "I believe I'm a woman trapped in a man's body." The doctor would have responded: "That's a problem with your mind. We need to bring your mind into alignment with your body."

If you go to a doctor today and make that same statement, the doctor in many places is likely to respond: "That's a problem with your body. Therefore we need to adjust your body — hormones, or surgery — in order to bring your physical reality as close to your psychological reality as possible."

What has to have happened between those two events for that shift to be possible? You could summarize it this way: inner psychological feelings have had to come to carry more authority than outward physical reality. The social imaginary has had to shift so that what you *feel* is now more authoritative than what you physically *are*. The central question of the transgender movement is simply: how does that shift take place? How do we reach a point where a feeling comes to have such decisive authority over identity?

There is a technological dimension to this — you can only give the second answer in an era when doctors can imagine they have the power to achieve the changes being requested. That requires hormone treatment and surgery. So technology is part of the story. But there is also a much broader and deeper story. It connects to a stream of ideas that has percolated through our culture over the last two or three centuries — ideas that have placed more and more emphasis on the inner self.

## The Erasure of External Frameworks

Consider the Psalms, or Paul's letters, or Augustine's *Confessions* — the great battles of soul described in those texts between the old nature and the new. What is striking is this: when we think of Psalm 73, or when we read Augustine, the inner space is explored only in order for the self to move *outward*. The movement in Psalm 73 is this: I was troubled by seeing the wicked prosper. Then I went into the sanctuary of God. There I could see reality as it truly is, and God's authoritative frame allowed me to reframe my own inner feelings.

The story of the last three hundred years is the story of the dismantling of all those external frameworks — family, church, nation — that once framed our identity by reaching inward and reorienting us. There is a reason Russian novels are so long and all their characters have patronymics: knowing who your father was was essential to knowing who *you* were. You were the son or daughter of someone. The same thing in the Bible — Elijah is "Elijah the Tishbite." You are not just yourself in isolation.

Today we have exiled those external markers. Family, religious institution, national identity — all are now in flux. One of the ways to tell this story is as the rise of the authority of the inner self. And the key figure here — not unique, but I think the most helpful — is the philosopher Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712–1778).

## Rousseau and the Two Foundational Ideas

Rousseau articulates two ideas that were broadly held in his age but now, through him, become the common property of the social imaginary.

**First**: the seat of identity is found in the inner psychological life of each individual. What we feel is the most important thing about us.

**Second**: society is corrupting. If the inner feelings are authoritative, then anything that surrounds or shapes or suppresses those inner feelings is inauthentic and oppressive. Society becomes the problem.

When Bruce Jenner (as he was then known) gave an interview to Diane Sawyer, he was speaking the language of Rousseau without knowing it. Rousseau called his autobiography the *Confessions* and opened it: "I am resolved on an undertaking that has no model and will have no imitator. I wish to show my fellow men a man in all the truth of nature; and this man shall be myself. I will present my inner self exactly as it was in every circumstance of my life." He is the first great modern to make the inner self the subject of a major work of literature.

And look how successful the Rousseau project has been in transforming society. Think about crime reporting today. We are always interested in the psychology of the criminal: why did he do it, what were his feelings, what were his motivations? Think about politicians who use profanity in public: why do they do that? Because when they swear, they sound *authentic*. They speak the private language we all recognize from before social pretensions shaped us. Think about celebrity culture: why do we want to know the sexual preferences of sports stars? Because those who are offended think that what you *are inwardly* is the real you. That's the Rousseau project, successfully installed in the popular imagination.

## Romanticism as the Popularizer

How do Rousseau's ideas become popularized? Through the movement known as Romanticism. Romanticism tried to connect people to their inner feelings and to nature rather than to society. Think of how Beethoven's music changes from his early to his late period — something shifts, as the older classical forms give way to a more emotional register. Look at Mary Wollstonecraft's letters from Scandinavia: nature is the true source of authentic feeling, she says, and the person who is in touch with their feelings — who is moved by the beauty and sublimity of nature — is the truly good person.

Wordsworth transforms the subject matter of poetry. The great poetry of the past was about great deeds — the *Iliad*, *Paradise Lost*. Then in 1800, Wordsworth publishes *Lyrical Ballads*, poems about ordinary rural scenes and humble people, designed to stir up emotions in order to make people more authentic. His poem "The Idiot Boy" is about a simple boy, and the point is: this is real human nature, unmediated by social convention and ambition. He is who he is. You might say today: no filter — what you see is what you get.

The implications are significant. First, notice what these Romantics are doing: they are using *art* to transform the way people imagine human life to be. Shelley referred to poets as "the unacknowledged legislators of the world." We might say today: the entertainers are the unacknowledged legislators of the world. It is the entertainment industry that shapes how we think about identity. Art and entertainment shape the social imaginary.

Second, if the inner self is authoritative and society is the problem, then traditional authorities become obstacles. Think about the dramatic collapse of confidence in traditional institutions over the last twenty to thirty years. Family, church, nation — all are in flux. Why? Because if what binds people together is the external framing institutions, and those are being eroded by the logic of expressive individualism, the capacity for external unity is destroyed.

## The Body as an Obstacle

And this is where we reach the transgender question. The trans movement is the latest expression of the logic of expressive individualism: you are your inner feelings. What is the last external constraint on the perfectly self-defining individual? The body. Think about the phrase "trapped in a body." That is fascinating. You are not trapped *in* your body — you *are* your body. There can be a very complicated relationship between brain and body, but the Christian view is that the person is body-and-soul together. The resurrection matters precisely because you are your body. When Paul rebukes the Corinthians' protological escape from the body toward a disembodied immortality, he is saying: no, that is not yet you. You are body and soul. That's you.

When you start to identify people with their feelings, even the body becomes potentially problematic. That is the position in which we find ourselves today.

## Questions and Answers

**Q: How do you avoid focusing on the inner self while living in Christian community, where vulnerability often involves sharing inner struggles?**

Good question. I do think one can overshare — the English instinct is not to air everything publicly. But I think the primary answer is not technique. Sitting under the regular proclamation of the Word — Luther talked about this, the Word comes from *outside* us and impacts and shapes us — being a regular member of the church in regular worship, that is transformative. And the Psalms: they are full of inner space, full of inner feelings, and yet they always — with the possible exception of Psalm 88 — draw inner feelings into connection with the external Word and external reality. The Psalms provide us with an excellent model of how to do this. They are, by definition, the perfect balance of introspection and reliance on the Word of God. That's why the Psalms should have a central place in every church's regular worship.

**Q: How do you counter this emotionally when rational arguments seem ineffective?**

It depends on the situation. One angle: secular statistics on life expectancy and health outcomes — from secular sources — can be presented as evidence that the lifestyle being promoted does not constitute flourishing. That is a kind of argument. But I would also say that love and community are powerful. Many people who have come out of the gay community and found Christ describe not being argued out of it but being *welcomed* into a genuine community. The first step is often not winning an argument but showing what genuine human community looks like.

**Q: Where do you disagree with the Romantic emphasis on the inner self?**

The inner space is real and important — Christians should attend to it. But the biblical notion of the self is consistently *other-directed*. You shall love the Lord your God — that is directed outward. You shall love your neighbor — outward. Sex in marriage, as Paul teaches it, is about the other person, not yourself. The social and cultural mandate is other-directed. That other-directedness is where I disagree with Rousseau and the Romantics. I am not a self-sufficient individual. From birth I am defined by utter dependence on others. I remain defined by relationships — husband, father, teacher — not by some autonomous inner core. The American preoccupation with Rogerian individualism needs to be carefully corrected by what Scripture actually teaches.
